title: Leave valid_to=null on legacy superseded records for time-travel visibility
context: When migrating v1 to bi-temporal v2, legacy superseded decisions lack a recorded successor instant. Previous logic set valid_to=date, creating zero-length [date,date) windows that matched no time-travel queries, hiding the records entirely. Touches invariant(s): con_902759b3dc (All JSON writes to .hunch/ must be atomic (temp-file + rename) so an interrupted write can never truncate the index); con_947c578b2c (Forward-migrate raw JSON to the current schema BEFORE Zod validation; never silently drop unmigratable records); con_a87360128b (Semantic vectors are a derived layer in SQLite, never the source of truth; reconcile by content hash on reindex); con_e04226bd05 (The MCP server must stay client-agnostic: all assistants point to the same .hunch/ graph; no Claude-only behavior in the server).
decision: For legacy superseded without a known successor instant, leave valid_to=null. This creates an open-ended [valid_from, null) window meaning 'in force from this date, end unknown.' A later `supersede` can set the real valid_to if/when it becomes known.
consequences: Legacy superseded records stay visible to all time-travel queries (queryable at any date >= valid_from)
Incomplete temporal information (unknown end-point) is naturally modeled as open vs. synthetically closed
Design history and rationale remain accessible for historical as-of queries
Future operations can record true valid_to when successor instant becomes known without data loss
alternatives_rejected: Set valid_to=date (zero-length window) — hides records from time-travel entirely
Drop records with unknown end-point — loses design history irretrievably
Use sentinel far-future date — obscures uncertainty and breaks later corrections
